data.title: 5 Alpha Generating Monsters
data.description:
Finding stocks that consistently generate positive alpha—meaning they outperform the market on a risk-adjusted basis—is a key goal for savvy investors. While markets can be unpredictable, certain companies have demonstrated a persistent ability to beat benchmarks through strong fundamentals, competitive advantages, and flawless execution. In this article, we highlight five standout stocks that have not only delivered market-beating returns but have also maintained positive alpha over extended periods, making them compelling choices for long-term portfolios.

Screen 1 – Sortino ratio

The Sortino ratio measures returns relative to downside risk. It’s a valuable metric because many investors are risk-averse, and when a stock has a history of large drawdowns, the Sortino ratio tends to be lower than it is for a stock with moderate drawdowns. A Sortino ratio of 1 indicates that the stock in question has experienced drawdowns that are roughly in line with its benchmark. A Sortino ratio higher than 1 indicates that when the benchmark declines, the stock declines to a lesser degree. This is the genesis of alpha.

I calculated the Sortino ratios for 250 stocks that had outperformed their benchmark over the last five years. I then eliminated those who scored less than 1.25. 83 stocks passed this screen.

Screen 2 – Alpha consistency

The second screen looked at how consistently these 83 stocks beat their benchmarks. I only considered those that generated positive alpha for 4 out of the last 5 years – from 2020 through 2024. The idea here was that any stock, no matter how much alpha it generates, can have a bad year, but I only wanted to include those who had one bad year out of the last five years. This screen yielded 71 names.

Screen 3 – Margin of Safety

The next screen looked at valuation. In most cases, stocks that deliver consistent alpha over a 5-year period are going to gain the attention of the analyst community and investors in general. They tend to trade at premium prices. So, to avoid overpaying for these stocks, I screened for the Margin of Safety, which is the difference between the stock’s market price and its fair value. For my purposes, I used a discounted cash flow method to arrive at fair value. I set the bar for the minimum MOS at 10%, which means that they are trading at a 10% discount. 36 stocks passed this screen.

Screen 4 – Earnings growth

In my last screen, I looked for positive earnings growth estimates for the next few years. I want to invest in companies that are expected to grow their earnings by a minimum of 10% per year. 28 stocks passed this screen.

The Results

For this article, I will highlight the top 5 stocks out of the 28 that passed all 4 screens. These stocks are growing their earnings at 10% or higher, are trading at reasonable prices, have shown consistency in generating alpha, and have good risk-adjusted returns. Here are the five stocks that I call Alpha Monsters.

Apollo Global Management, Inc. (APO)

  • Sector: Financial Services.
  • Industry: Asset Management.
  • Specialties: Private Equity, Credit, Real Assets, Retirement Services.
  • AUM: $650 billion.
  • 4 out of 5 positive alpha years.
  • 1.31 Sortino ratio.
  • 32% Margin of Safety.
  • 22.2% 5-year annual earnings growth estimate.
  • Seeking Alpha analysts rating: Buy.

Coca-Cola Consolidated, Inc. (COKE)

  • Sector: Consumer Defensive.
  • Industry: Beverages – Non-Alcoholic.
  • Market Cap: $10 billion.
  • 4 out of 5 positive alpha years.
  • 1.76 Sortino ratio.
  • 26% Margin of Safety.
  • 47.3% 5-year annual earnings growth estimate.
  • Seeking Alpha analysts rating: Buy.

Enova International, Inc. (ENVA)

  • Sector: Financial Services.
  • Industry: Credit Services.
  • 5-year performance: outperformed the market by 400%.
  • 4 out of 5 positive alpha years.
  • 1.46 Sortino ratio.
  • 37% Margin of Safety.
  • 38.7% 5-year annual earnings growth estimate.
  • Seeking Alpha analysts rating: Buy.

Fair Isaac Corporation (FICO)

  • Sector: Technology.
  • Industry: Software – Application.
  • Market Cap: $42 billion.
  • Founded in 1956.
  • Lifetime CAGR: 20.9%.
  • 4 out of 5 positive alpha years.
  • 1.78 Sortino ratio.
  • 43% Margin of Safety.
  • 26.6% 5-year annual earnings growth estimate.
  • Seeking Alpha analysts rating: Hold.

Eli Lilly and Company (APO)

  • Sector: Healthcare.
  • Industry: Pharmaceuticals.
  • Specialties: diabetes, oncology, immunology, neuroscience.
  • Founded in 1876.
  • 5 out of 5 positive alpha years (perfect score).
  • 2.32 Sortino ratio.
  • 22% Margin of Safety.
  • 25.1% 5-year annual earnings growth estimate.
  • Seeking Alpha analysts rating: Buy.

Final Thoughts

These 5 stocks are a rare breed. They produce consistently positive alpha; they’re growing earnings; they are not overpriced; and they hold up well when the market goes down. So, what should you do with these 5 names? I can think of a few things.

First, don’t rush out and buy them based solely on this article. As always, do your own investigation into any company that looks good at first glance. Second, if one or more of them pass your scrutiny, look into how they will impact your overall asset allocation. If you’re already overweight in Financial Services, for instance, you don’t need two more names there. But you might be able to do an upgrade by selling something you’re holding now and buying something new that has better numbers.

And third, if you listen to the 45% of economists who believe that a recession is likely within the next 12 months, the market will probably go back down and re-test the April low. If you are worried that this may happen, just put these names on your watch list and see if you can pick up a name or two at significantly better prices.

On the other hand, if you’re convinced that the worst is over and the market is headed for new highs, then do your research, vet these names, and buy the ones that pass your tests.

data.title: Nickel Miners News For The Month Of May 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the nickel miners news for May.

The past month saw nickel prices slightly lower and LME inventory slightly lower.

Nickel price news

As of May 30, the nickel spot price was $15,354/t, slightly lower from $15,544/tonne last month.

Nickel inventory at the London Metals Exchange [LME] was slightly lower the past month at 200,862 tonnes (201,426 tonnes last month).

Nickel spot price 10 year chart - Current price = $15,354/t (source)

Nickel demand vs. supply chart

S&P Global Market Intelligenceforecastst nickel oversupply to 2027, then a 'primary' nickel deficit starting in 2028 (as of June 2024) (source)

Carbon Credits forecasts a nickel oversupply to 2030, then growing deficits from 2030-2035 (as of May 2025) (source)

Trend Investing vs. IEA demand forecast for EV metals (Trend Investing) (IEA)

2022 — IEA forecasts 60 new nickel mines needed by 2030

Lithium & Nickel are the two 'high importance to energy' critical materials in the medium term (2025-2035) (source - page 29)

Nickel Market News

On May 21, Fastmarkets reported:

Nickel prices stagnate amid oversupply and limited supply response from Indonesia...Data from the International Nickel Study Group points to the nickel market remaining heavily oversubscribed in early 2025, and Fastmarkets forecasts a third consecutive annual market surplus above 100,000 tonnes in 2025. With such fundamentals, it is difficult to envision any sustained recovery in the nickel price unless demand growth accelerates, or the supply side shows greater discipline.

On May 22, Discovery Alert reported:

Nickel price forecast: Key factors driving 2025 market dynamics...Despite widespread discussion of market surpluses, exchange inventories have shown a notable decline in recent weeks. This contradiction between market narrative and actual inventory levels suggests underlying strength in consumption patterns that may not be fully recognized in current nickel price forecast models...LME and SHFE warehouses have reported a combined 8% reduction in nickel inventories over the past six weeks... Indonesian ore prices have risen to near two-year highs, approaching $48 per wet metric tonne for 1.8% nickel content... "Indonesia has both the means and motivation to support higher nickel prices. At current levels, some of the country's less efficient producers are barely breaking even, creating pressure for government intervention in mining"...

Producers

On May 21, Nornickel announced: "More power, less emissions: Nornickel completes modernization of CHP-2 in Talnakh."

On May 22, Nornickel announced: "Nornickel’s board of directors recommends not to pay dividends for FY 2024 to shareholders."

Vale Voisey's Bay Mine is a key Canadian and global source of nickel. Vale plans a refinery at Bécancour in Québec to supply GM with battery grade nickel sulfate with deliveries targeted to commence in H2 2026.

On May 12, Market Screener reported: "CICC initiates Vale S.A. at outperform with $11.30 Price Target."

On May 16, PT Vale Indonesia announced:

PT Vale Indonesia Tbk holds 2025 Annual General Meeting of shareholders...The year 2024 was a transformational year for PT Vale. Despite global economic headwinds and declining nickel prices, the Company recorded resilient performance...The Company recorded the lowest cash cost of sales in three years at US$9,374 per ton nickel...

On May 22, Market Screener reported: "Brazil's Vale approves issuance of 6 billion reais in local debt."

Conclusion

Nickel spot prices were slightly lower the last month.

Highlights for the month were:

  • Nickel prices stagnate amid oversupply and limited supply response from Indonesia.
  • LME and SHFE warehouses have reported a combined 8% reduction in nickel inventories over the past six weeks. Indonesian ore prices have risen to near two-year highs, approaching $48 per wet metric tonne for 1.8% nickel content.
  • Glencore Q1 2025 own sourced nickel production of 18,800t was in line with Q1 2024.
  • BHP launches its first Industry AI Hub in Singapore to accelerate AI adoption in the mining and resources sector.
  • Danantara Indonesia and INA announce Strategic Partnership with Eramet to explore the establishment of a strategic investment platform in the nickel sector, spanning upstream to downstream operations.
  • Sherritt International Q1 Adjusted net loss from continuing operations was $22.8 million or $(0.06) per share. Phase two of the Moa JV expansion was substantially completed.
  • IGO Limited reports strong quarterly operating and financial result at Nova in Q1, 2025.
  • Jinchuan Group International Resources is working to remedy the issues, will remain suspended until further notice.
  • Sumitomo Metal Mining's profit plunges 72% in Fiscal 2024.
  • Nickel 28 Ramu Q1 production of 648 tonnes of contained cobalt in MHP, compared to 767 tonnes in the same period last year.
  • Talon Metals drills record grades of 23.28% NiEq or 48.87% CuEq over 8.25 meters outside of the Tamarack Resource Area.
  • Premium Resources drilling extends mineralization 315 metres below the resource area, demonstrating scaled expansion at the Selebi Project.
  • Canada Nickel - Continued exploration success at Reid, Mann West and Deloro.

data.title: Dell: It's Not Too Late To Buy
data.description:
Performance Assessment

My bullish view on Dell Technologies (DELL) has been playing out nicely as the stock has outperformed the broader market index:

Thesis

Dell reported its Q1 FY26 earnings (it has a Jan-ending fiscal year) yesterday. Here's how I'm viewing the stock now:

  1. We can expect meaningful growth acceleration driven by a large AI server shipments backlog
  2. Dell is well positioned to gain share in the PC refresh cycle
  3. The PC refresh cycle can lead to sequential improvement in margins
  4. There is some valuation risk as the stock is trading higher than usual vs peers
  5. DELL looks like it will continue to outperform the S&P500

We Can Expect Meaningful Growth Acceleration Driven By A Large AI Server Shipments Backlog

Dell's Client Solutions Group [CSG] sells PCs to consumers and enterprises. Its Infrastructure Solutions Group [ISG] sells servers and storage solutions to enterprises. Together, these 2 segments make up almost 98% of the overall revenue mix. The company expects this to see meaningful acceleration in Q2 FY26 with 19% YoY growth:

This is due largely to explosive growth in AI server shipments:

In ISG, [for Q2] we expect to ship roughly $7 billion of AI servers as we fulfill some of our large deals.

- CFO Yvonne McGill in the Q1 FY26 earnings call

For the full year FY26, Dell expects to ship more than $15 billion worth of AI servers, which corresponds to more than 50% YoY growth:

In the Q1 press release, management's commentary was very encouraging as the backlog is made up of multiple elements from a wide variety of demand sources

...we’re experiencing unprecedented demand for our AI-optimized servers…We generated $12.1 billion in AI orders this quarter alone, surpassing the entirety of shipments in all of FY25 and leaving us with $14.4 billion in backlog...It's [The backlog] filled through lots of multiple types of technology, versions of Blackwell, and it now contains software... I like our prospects of converting more pipeline in the second half.

- COO Jeffrey Clarke in the Q1 FY26 Press Release and Q1 FY26 earnings call

Dell's opportunity pipeline is currently at multiples of the backlog. I deduce that this means at least $43 billion of potential business; a very good sign for future revenue prospects. Thus, it makes sense that the company's revenue guidance of $29 billion at the mid-point for Q2 surpassed consensus expectations by almost 15%:

Dell Is Well Positioned To Gain Share In The PC Refresh Cycle

On the PC side, Dell is steadily gaining market share in recent quarters (grey line in the chart below):

Now, there is a PC refresh cycle catalyst coming in:

We are in the PC refresh cycle, as we are seeing clear indications that the install base is upgrading to new Windows 11 PCs, many of them AI PCs

- Q1 FY26 Earnings Presentation

I expect Dell to outperform its peers and gain further market share in this cycle as they have a lead in AI PCs that is synchronizing well with their own new product launches:

...we lead the industry with the broadest portfolio of Copilot+ capable AI PCs shipping since March. Strategically timed with the Windows 11 refresh, this allows our customers to move to the latest technology and future proof for on-device AI workloads. We announced new Dell Pro Max notebooks and desktops equipped with NVIDIA RTX PRO Blackwell GPUs, Intel Core Ultra processors, and AMD Ryzen and Threadripper processors.

- COO Jeffrey Clarke in the Q1 FY26 earnings call

The PC Refresh Cycle Can Lead To Sequential Improvement In Margins

Dell has started FY26 with a 340bps sequential hit to gross profit margins:

This was due to lower prices from increased competitive pressures and also some unfavorable geographic mix in traditional server sales. The gross margin hit here also flowed through to EBIT margins, resulting in a 72 bps miss vs consensus estimates:

But it looks like the upcoming quarters may fare better as new AI PC launches at higher average selling prices (ASPs) is expected to provide some margins lift:

In CSG, we expect the PC refresh cycle to continue as the install base upgrades to new devices, resulting in improved profitability sequentially.

- CFO Yvonne McGill in the Q1 FY26 earnings call

There Is Some Valuation Risk As The Stock Is Trading Higher Than Usual Vs. Peers

DELL stock is trading at a 1-yr FWD P/E of 11.4x; a little bit above the median PE of its comps:

DELL's 8.2% premium vs comps is a little bit higher than the typical 5.1% premium it has traded at usually:

And actually this is understating the real premium as it does not account for the 3.5% after-hours pop in DELL stock following the earnings release yesterday and the +0.92% pre-market pop today. So overall, I think there is some valuation risk here. But I am willing to take that on given the great revenue growth prospects and a likely bottom in gross margins.

DELL Looks Like It Will Continue To Outperform The S&P500

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of DELL vs SPX500

The DELL vs SPX500 monthly charts look bullish as the ratio prices have printed a bullish engulfing candle after a failed breakout below a key monthly support level. So I expect upside momentum and hence outperformance vs the broader market to continue.

Takeaway & Positioning

My bullish view on DELL has been playing out well, and I continue being buy-biased after the company's Q1 FY26 earnings release. DELL posted an impressive ~15% revenue guidance beat for Q2 driven by high demand for its AI servers, for which the backlog is nearly $15 billion and pipeline multiples bigger than that. On the PC side, DELL seems well positioned to continue outperforming its peers amid an upcoming PC refresh cycle as the company leads in AI PCs. The technical charts also suggest continued outperformance for DELL over the S&P500.

The main risk I see is that the stock is valued higher than normal vs its peers. But this is a risk I am willing to accept given the promising revenue outlook for FY26 and likely bottom in gross margins.

Rating: 'Buy'

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Neutral/hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: DMO: Don't Be Fooled By The High Yield
data.description:
The Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund Inc (NYSE:DMO) is a closed-end fund that allows investors to earn an attractive level of income by investing in mortgage-backed securities. The fund does quite well in this respect, as its current 13.45% yield is substantially higher than any of the major domestic fixed-income indices. We can see that here:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG)

3.83%

SPDR® Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK)

6.63%

iShares MBS ETF (MBB)

4.10%

One of the major problems with many domestic bond indices is that they include a large number of bonds that were issued during the zero-interest-rate era that are trading at prices far below their face value and still have very low coupon yields. The website for the iShares MBS ETF, which tracks the Bloomberg U.S. MBS Index, states that the index has a weighted average coupon of 3.41% right now:

This is far below the current market rate on a thirty-year residential mortgage, which is 7.01% according to Zillow:

The presence of all the old mortgage-backed securities that were issued back when interest rates were much lower is therefore dragging down the yield of the indices themselves. This is disappointing because the current 4.10% yield on the Bloomberg U.S. MBS Index does not provide enough income to provide for the needs of a typical investor. At that yield, a $1 million investment will produce $41,000 per year in annual income. It is fairly safe to say that most people who have managed to amass that level of wealth will want a more luxurious lifestyle than can be provided with that level of income. The Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund obviously provides such a way to get a higher level of income from investing in mortgages, as its yield kicks the annual income generated by a $1 million investment up to $134,500 annually. The fund is also actively managed, so it does not necessarily have the older low-yielding securities dragging down its payout. Of course, this certainly does not mean that the fund is without risk, and it certainly does not mean that the distribution is sustainable. Let us take a closer look at it in order to determine whether or not it could be right for our income portfolios today.

As just shown, the current yield of the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund is substantially higher than that of the indices. It also compares fairly well with its peers, as shown here:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund

Fixed Income – Taxable-High Yield

13.45%

Allspring Income Opportunities Fund (EAD)

Fixed Income – Taxable-High Yield

9.45%

BNY Mellon High Yield Strategies Fund (DHF)

Fixed Income – Taxable-High Yield

8.40%

BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund, Inc (HYT)

Fixed Income – Taxable-High Yield

9.69%

Credit Suisse High Yield Bond (DHY)

Fixed Income – Taxable-High Yield

9.12%

KKR Income Opportunities Fund (KIO)

Fixed Income – Taxable-High Yield

12.16%

Neuberger Berman High Yield Strategies (NHS)

Fixed Income – Taxable-High Yield

14.70%

As we can see, the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund has a substantially higher yield than most of its peers. A legitimate argument could be made over how closely some of these peers compare to the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund. Morningstar puts this fund in the same category as junk bond funds, but many mortgage-backed securities are actually investment-grade securities. For example, take a look at the credit quality breakdown of the iShares MBS ETF:

As we can see, the mortgage-backed securities index is comprised exclusively of AA-rated bonds, which is an investment-grade rating. All agency mortgage-backed securities are investment-grade securities because they have either the explicit or implicit backing of the United States government, and that government still has an investment-grade credit rating. However, there are some mortgage-backed securities in the market that have lower ratings. As we might surmise from this fund’s classification, it includes a significant allocation to these non-investment-grade mortgages. We can see that by looking at the credit quality breakdown of the securities in the fund:

The fund officially has 6.95% of its assets invested in investment-grade securities, if we exclude the 3.30% invested in cash and cash equivalents (which are generally considered investment-grade as well). We can see that the bulk of its assets are invested in unrated securities, which can technically be either investment-grade or speculative-grade assets. However, in most cases, unrated securities are below investment-grade simply because most issuers that could achieve and deserve an investment-grade rating will actually get their issuances rated in order to save money on interest. Thus, while technically the fund might be invested in investment-grade assets like the mortgage-backed securities indices, it is probably holding lower-quality debt. This could be a contributing factor to the fund’s very high yield, and it also means that the fund is more comparable to a junk bond fund than it is to an investment-grade bond fund. There are very few closed-end funds that focus specifically on mortgage-backed securities, so comparing this fund to junk bond funds is probably the most accurate way to do a peer comparison. As we can see, the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund compares quite well to them in terms of yield. Unfortunately, though, the very high yield relative to peers could mean that the market is pricing in a distribution cut. We should take a closer look at it to determine what the risk of that might be.

Performance Analysis

At their core, mortgage-backed securities behave very much like bonds. The owner of a certain piece of real estate makes the monthly payment on their mortgage loan, and in most cases, the interest rate on the mortgage is fixed over the lifetime of the loan. The money that the servicer receives via the real estate owner’s payments is pooled together with the payments of lots of other real estate owners who are making payments on their mortgages. This money is then used to pay the holders of the mortgage-backed securities. Owners of these securities, therefore, receive a regular payment just like any other bond. The price of these securities can be expected to move similarly to that of bonds (inversely to interest rates), with the magnitude of such movements depending on the duration of the mortgage-backed securities.

Bonds in general have performed fairly poorly in recent years due mostly to the surge in interest rates after the COVID-19 pandemic. Over the past three years, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is down 5.43%, the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index is down 2.86%, and the Bloomberg U.S. MBS Index is down 6.80%:

The Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund invests primarily in mortgage-backed securities, just like the Bloomberg U.S. MBS Index. However, as we just saw, the mortgages that the fund invests in appear to be of lower quality than those of the index. As such, they could be expected to have a higher yield, which in most cases means that the duration is shorter. Thus, we can probably expect the securities in the fund’s portfolio to have delivered a performance that is somewhere between that of the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index and the Bloomberg U.S. MBS Index.

This does not, however, mean that the fund itself will have delivered a performance in between the two indices. The fund employs a significant amount of leverage (47.12% according to CEF Data), which would amplify its movements in either direction. In addition, the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund is a closed-end fund, so its share price will not necessarily perfectly track the value of its assets.

With that said, this fund actually did perform somewhat as we expected. Over the past three years, its share price has declined by 4.20%:

The fund’s share price performance was actually roughly between that of the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index and the Bloomberg U.S. MBS Index, which is pretty much what we expected. However, we can see that it was far more volatile than either index, which was undoubtedly due to its very high level of leverage. An investor seeking a degree of safety might not like the volatility here, but overall, the fund’s price performance was not really too bad.

On a total return basis, the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund substantially outperformed all the indices over the trailing three-year period:

As shown, the fund delivered a 38.05% total return, which was more than double the 18.22% total return of the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index. Its performance against the other indices was even more dramatic than this. It is not surprising that this should be the case, as the much higher yield of this fund relative to the index provided its total return with a much larger boost than was the case for any of the indices.

The strong total return performance of the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund also means that this fund actually managed to deliver a positive real return over the past three years, which was not the case for two of the three indices:

This chart shows the total real returns that an investor in each of the four assets would have earned if that investor reinvested all the distributions paid out by the fund and the exchange-traded funds tracking the three indices. Furthermore, the total return figures have been adjusted downward based on the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers. Thus, if that index accurately measures inflation, then the above figures reflect the gain or loss of purchasing power that an investor would have experienced over the past three years. As we can see, only the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund and the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index managed to preserve the purchasing power of an investor’s capital under those conditions. This is very important for retirees and near-retirees because a failure to maintain the purchasing power of their investment principal will result in a decreasing quality of life over time. Obviously, nobody wants this, so it is best to maintain exposure to assets that should preserve purchasing power over time. The Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund has managed to do this better than either investment-grade bonds or investment-grade mortgage-backed securities over the past three years.

About The Fund

The fund’s website states that the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a high level of current income. This makes sense given the composition of the fund’s portfolio, which, as the website explains, comprises:

Non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities, commercial mortgage-backed securities, and mortgage whole loans.

As mentioned earlier, these securities are essentially a special type of bond and bonds by their nature are income vehicles. In fact, any bond held over its lifetime will deliver only current income because bonds are issued and redeemed at face value. Thus, current income makes the most sense as an objective for any bond fund.

Investors can expect most of this fund’s returns to come via the distributions as opposed to through the appreciation of the share price. As we can see here, over the past ten years, the share price of the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund has declined by 53.21%, whereas the fund’s investors have actually received a 58.83% total return:

This is possible because of this fund’s incredibly high yield. Basically, what occurred over the trailing ten-year period is that the fund paid out sufficient amounts of money via its distribution to completely offset the share price decline and still deliver a positive total return. Thus, in this case, investors still made money overall even though the share price fell a lot. Unfortunately, though, anyone who purchased the fund with the intent of receiving an income that they can spend to support themselves has still suffered a massive loss of capital. It would have been necessary to reinvest some of the distributions that were received in order to keep the size of the position stable. Many investors who are looking for a very high level of income may not want to do that since it reduces the effective yield that they can spend. It could also represent a bigger problem in that the fund might be paying out more than it can really afford.

A look at the fund’s net asset value reinforces that it could be paying out more than it can afford. This chart shows the net asset value per share of the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund over the past three years:

As we can see, the fund’s net asset value has declined by 13.02% over the past three years. This is a substantially worse performance than the fund’s share price delivered over the same period, which we saw earlier in this article. Indeed, the share price has held up quite well, and that could give investors a very misleading opinion of how well the fund’s portfolio is actually doing.

As many investors know, the bond market has been under pressure recently, particularly at the long end. As I showed in a recent article, yields for the ten-year, twenty-year, and thirty-year U.S. Treasury notes and bonds have risen since the start of April. This is occurring despite the assurances from the Federal Reserve that it will be reducing interest rates during the second half of next year, and in both 2026 and 2027:

This chart shows the latest projections for the year-end effective federal funds rate through 2027. As it shows, the Federal Open Market Committee predicts that the effective federal funds rate will be at 3.9% by the end of 2025. That would require two 25-basis-point cuts from the current level. Furthermore, we see that the central bank expects further cuts in 2026 and 2027. Despite this, yields on long-dated U.S. Treasuries are rising. I have speculated in previous articles that this could be due to the market doubting that the United States will be able to avoid debt monetization, which would result in investors getting paid back with a currency that is worth less than what was originally lent. In order to compensate for this, investors demand higher yields in an attempt to make sure that they get a positive real return.

This is important because the price of mortgage-backed securities correlates directly with the yield of long-term U.S. Treasuries. Take a look at this chart showing the performance of the Bloomberg U.S. MBS Index against the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF) over the past five years:

As we can see, the two asset classes exhibited a very high level of correlation. This makes sense because mortgages are debts that are paid back over an extended period of time. Thus, when the yields on the ten-year U.S. Treasury note rise, we can expect mortgage rates to rise as well.

This could prove to be somewhat problematic for the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund if it continues to be the case. After all, an increase in the yield of U.S. Treasury securities will likely push up mortgage rates and push down the price of mortgage-backed securities. The fund’s net asset value has already declined 1.75% year-to-date, as shown here:

Thus, the fund has already suffered losses and failed to cover its distributions year-to-date. Further weakness in bond prices, corresponding to rising yields, will likely increase the fund’s losses and further pressure the distribution.

Another potential risk here comes from the fact that the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund invests in both residential and commercial mortgages. As of right now, the fund is mostly exposed to residential mortgages, but its exposure to commercial mortgages is not negligible:

We can see here that the fund has 16.75% of its assets invested in “Commercial Mortgages – Large Loan Credit (SASB),” 3.26% of its assets invested in “Commercial Mortgages – Conduit Credit,” and 2.65% of its assets invested in “Commercial Mortgages – Agency CMBS.” That is 22.66% of the fund’s assets invested in these three types of commercial mortgages and commercial mortgage-backed securities. The reason this is important is that real estate developers and owners who have commercial mortgages against their properties have been defaulting at a higher rate than in past years. CRE Daily published an article on this late last year, and it included this chart from the Financial Times:

We can see that the sheer size of the defaults increased dramatically in 2023 and 2024 compared to previous levels. This could be set to continue to increase going forward, according to some sources. According to CRE Analyst, the total volume of commercial mortgage maturities in 2024 was $950 billion, which will increase to $1 trillion in 2025, and rise to as high as $1.26 trillion in 2027. The owners of the real estate against which these mortgages are written will have to refinance all of this debt as it comes due, and this will probably result in them being forced to do so at a much higher rate of interest than was previously the case. Depending on the cash flows of these buildings, it is possible that the higher interest rates could make the mortgages unaffordable after the rollover, prompting a wave of defaults. It should be easy to see how that would negatively impact the fund since defaults on a mortgage will flow through to the owners of any mortgage-backed securities connected to that mortgage. In other words, the fund will suffer losses if the borrower defaults. Thus, an increase in mortgage defaults could cause further damage to the net asset value of this fund. There is no reason to expect that interest rates on commercial mortgages will come down to the level that we saw during the 2010 to 2021 period when most of these loans were initially made, so this is a very real risk.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund is to provide its investors with a very high level of current income. In accordance with that objective, it pays a monthly distribution of $0.1300 per share ($1.56 per share annually). This gives it a whopping 13.45% yield at the current share price.

The fund has maintained its current distribution since the middle of 2024, but it has been steadily increasing it since mid-2022:

This does not necessarily mean that the distribution is sustainable, however. As we have seen throughout this article, the fund’s net asset value has been declining, suggesting that it cannot afford to distribute as much money as it actually is. Let us take a look at the fund’s most recent financial statements to get an idea of how well it is actually covering the distribution.

The most recent financial report for the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund is the annual report for the full-year period that ended on December 31, 2024. Let us take a look at it now.

For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund reported a net investment income of $13,168,718. That was not sufficient to cover the $17,360,594 that the fund paid out.

The fund reported a net realized loss of $11,368,993 for the full-year period. Obviously, then, the net investment income plus the net realized gains was not sufficient to cover the distribution since that only works out to $1,799,725. The fund likewise did not have sufficient net unrealized gains to make up the difference:

As clearly shown here, the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund distributed more money in both 2023 and 2024 than it earned from its investment portfolio. This reinforces my earlier point that the fund seems to be consistently failing to fully cover its distributions. This is concerning, as no fund can continue to do this indefinitely. The fund will almost certainly have to cut the payout at some point.

Valuation

Shares of the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund are currently trading at a 1.35% discount to net asset value. This is very slightly better than the 1.18% discount that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month.

Over the past three years, shares of the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund have traded at prices ranging from a 16.97% discount to a 0.57% premium to net asset value. The average price over the period was a 7.39% discount. Obviously, the fund is significantly more expensive today than it has been on average over the past three years.

Here is how the current price of the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund compares to its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund

(1.35%)

Allspring Income Opportunities Fund

(6.06%)

BNY Mellon High Yield Strategies Fund

(9.12%)

BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund

1.37%

Credit Suisse High Yield Bond Fund

(5.12%)

KKR Income Opportunities Fund

(4.72%)

Neuberger Berman High Yield Strategies Fund

(0.67%)

The Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund is not the most expensive fund out of its peer group. As we can see, there is one fund that is currently trading at a premium and one other fund that has a smaller discount on net asset value. Thus, this fund does not appear to be excessively expensive compared to its peers. However, it still seems rather pricey given that it is fairly consistently failing to cover its distribution. It is also fairly expensive compared to its three-year average.

In short, the fund seems rather expensive right now. As it is failing to cover its distribution, it may be better to try to get it at a larger discount on net asset value in order to add a margin of safety. The fund’s long-term history suggests that it might be possible to obtain a larger discount at some point in the future.

Expenses

The Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund has a whopping 8.07% expense ratio:

As is the case with many closed-end funds, though, the majority of the fund’s expenses come from the fact that it has to pay interest on the money that it has borrowed in order to obtain leverage. If we exclude these expenses, the fund’s expense ratio drops to 2.16%. The interest expenses alone here are substantial, which is a direct function of the high level of leverage that this fund runs.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Western Asset Mortgage Opportunity Fund boasts a very high yield that is certain to attract some investors who want to earn a high level of income from their portfolios. However, the risks here are higher than might be expected from a mortgage-backed securities fund, as the fund seems to mostly be investing in below-investment-grade mortgages. These securities have a higher risk of default than most agency mortgages, and this is especially true for the commercial mortgages that the fund holds. In addition, it appears to be consistently failing to cover its distribution, and the payouts are destroying the fund’s net asset value.

data.title: Weitz Conservative Allocation Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The Conservative Allocation Fund's Institutional Class returned -0.24% for the first quarter compared to +1.95% for the Morningstar Moderately Conservative Target Risk Index.

The Fund’s bond holdings posted positive contributions for the quarter from coupon income and price appreciation, across all fixed income sectors. The Fund's stock holdings declined modestly during the quarter, though equity results handily outperformed the Russell 3000 Index, which was down -4.72% for the quarter. International stocks and long-duration Treasury bonds topped the performance charts for the quarter. We don’t own much of either, by design, which helps explain the Fund’s relative shortfall to the index.

The first quarter was filled with crosscurrents, mixed signals, and brewing volatility. The year began with a strong equity rally in January, driven by investor enthusiasm about a perceived pro-growth agenda from the new administration and the promise of continued advances in generative artificial intelligence (“AI”). Those early gains evaporated in February and March as investors began questioning the returns on the gargantuan capital spending needed to drive the AI revolution. Things got more complicated in Washington and it turned out that 1Q was just a prelude to the tariff trauma that would come in April.

Notable 1Q Contributors and Detractors

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), Aon (AON), Visa (V), Roper Technologies (ROP), and Linde (LIN) were the Fund’s leading quarterly contributors, with all five stocks posting double-digit quarterly returns. These are durable businesses run by highly capable managers. The companies have methodically grown business value per share over extended time frames, both organically and via astute capital deployment. While they are not immune from economic ups and downs, they have all found ways to weather tough times and thrive through cycles.

IDEX (IEX), Danaher (DHR), Accenture (ACN), Bio-Techne (TECH), and Alphabet (GOOG) were the Fund’s largest quarterly detractors. Life sciences and bioprocessing stocks were broadly marked down as a long-awaited cyclical recovery was muted by a string of headlines about potential new macro headwinds. Technology-adjacent stocks were another notable pocket of weakness during the quarter. For example, Accenture’s stock fell as the Department of Government Efficiency (“DOGE”) took aim at federal contracts with consulting companies. In each case, we think the stock price reactions far exceeded any impact on the underlying businesses.

1Q Trading Activity

We initiated a new position in Equifax during the first quarter. Equifax is best known as one of three primary U.S. credit reporting agencies, but the company’s crown jewel is the Equifax Workforce Solutions (EWS) business. The core business of EWS is Verification Services, which is used for mortgage origination, pre-employment background screening, and government benefit applications. The EWS business generally grows at a healthy rate with high margins, a long unit expansion runway, and pricing power. We think Equifax is underearning today due to depressed mortgage volumes and weak labor market hiring. As these end markets stabilize, our view is that revenue and cash flows can accelerate meaningfully.

We added to the Fund’s equity positions in IDEX, Danaher, Accenture, and Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) at increasingly attractive prices. We modestly trimmed the Fund’s holdings of Berkshire Hathaway as the stock’s position size ballooned after meaningful outperformance. We also exited the Fund’s position in Honeywell International (HON) at healthy, though not exceptional, gains. In bonds, we continued to layer in 2-to-5-year Treasuries to lock in yields in the 4.0% to 4.5% range.

Looking Forward

The Fund's overall portfolio continues to evolve with market conditions. We have said for some time that stocks and other “risk-on” assets do not seem cheap, and our positioning reflects that view. We own common equity stakes in 26 companies totaling 41.1% of net assets. The fixed income portfolio includes securitized debt (14.2%), investment-grade corporate bonds (0.2%), Treasury securities (35.0%), and cash equivalents/other (9.5%). We have ample resources to invest heavily in new opportunities as our team uncovers them.

We think the investment landscape for allocation investors is well-balanced for those with tempered expectations. In our view, the Fund's securities offer adequate long-term capital appreciation potential. High-quality bonds yielding more than 4% have enhanced the current income outlook. data.title: Nebius Is Still Undervalued After The Surge
data.description:
In the AI arms race, few are growing as rapidly or visibly as Nebius Group (NBIS). Its Q1 2025 results reported a nearly 700% YoY growth in annualized revenue run rate [ARR], to $310 million in April. With the support of Nvidia (NVDA) and recent placement in SemiAnalysis’s golden tier of GPU Cloud ClusterMAX rankings, the company has drawn comparisons to early AWS and CoreWeave (CRWV). But with the increasingly commoditized and crowded AI infrastructure market, rapid growth is not enough to ensure value creation that endures.

Since my initial thesis on Nebius identified it as a deep-value AI infrastructure play, the stock has surged over 80% in just two months, validating the market’s overdue recognition of its strategic position. While the sharp rerating has captured early upside, Nebius still trades at an implied ~16x ARR (based on April's $310 million run rate), a discount to peers like CoreWeave when adjusted for growth velocity and vertical integration, suggesting further upside may unfold over the next few quarters as revenue durability and software monetization improve.

Nebius’s ARR Is Soaring, But Is It Built to Last?

Nebius has recorded one of the strongest revenue growth stories in the AI infrastructure market. Having recorded only $90 million of annualized run-rate revenue [ARR] at the end of 2024, the company increased to $250 million in March 2025 and then to $310 million in April.

This is almost 700% growth from the previous year. The acceleration is being fueled by the growing demand for GPU compute from technology firms and AI startups, which are in a hurry to run and deploy big models. Based on the interest generated by events such as the release of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) R1 in February, Nebius has managed to ramp capacity in key regions such as New Jersey, Kansas City, and Iceland quickly.

Yet, this growth, impressive as it is, might not be as stable as it seems. A good part of the revenue seems to be driven by short-term GPU use by newer companies, many of which still rely on venture money. These sorts of revenue streams from short-term customers are not as stable as the kind of sales from multi-year, multi-million-dollar deals with big companies.

The firm has not yet reported how much of its ARR is recurring and how concentrated it is among a few customers, so it is difficult to say how stable this revenue actually is. Many of the software solutions that Nebius provides, such as enhanced storage, orchestration software, and developer integrations, are packaged as part of infrastructure deals and not sold as add-ons, which restricts revenue diversification.

In the future, Nebius is setting its sights high with its guidance of $750 million to $1 billion in year-end ARR. Achieving that goal will take more than hardware expansion, it will take more profound engagement with enterprise customers, more repeatable software-driven revenue, and improved visibility on contract terms. The early trends are good, but the second leg of growth will prove how sturdy and repeatable this model is.

Burn Now, Margin Later: Nebius’s $2B Bet On AI Infrastructure

The firm's revenue today is predominantly driven by infrastructure services, with no material segment disclosure in the earnings call or 20-F filing. This growth-intensive foundation necessitates persistent GPU deployment and data center expansions to serve demand sustainably. In the first quarter of the year alone, Nebius spent $544 million on CapEx and is forecasting spending of ~$2 billion for full-year 2025, higher than prior guidance of $1.5 billion. However, its cash balance as of Q1 was only $1.44 billion, which would mean that the remainder of the year's CapEx will need to be partially covered by monetizing non-core equity positions or dilutive fundraise.

Even with this heavy spending, adjusted EBITDA is projected to remain negative in 2025, break-even to target in H2, and profitability of the infrastructure segment to target in Q3. EBIT is projected to achieve 20–30% in the midterm, but this is based on scaling software and growing inference workloads, which tend to have higher utilization and margin.

The more extended-term thesis relies on the ability of the AI Cloud layer, consisting of orchestration software, storage optimization, and multi-platform integrations, to transform from a bundled cost center to a money-generating, high-margin software business. Currently, software revenue is still minimal, and the majority of customer interactions are centered on capacity more so than platform differentiation.

Why Nebius’s Growth Needs More Than Startup Demand

One of Nebius's advantages is its contract flexibility, providing pay-as-you-go and short-term reserve offerings, which have enabled speedy uptake among cash-starved AI startups. However, the same flexibility introduces uncertainty. Contracts tend to last from a few months to one year, with none of the multi-year reserved capacity deals that CoreWeave and Oracle Cloud Infrastructure have utilized to secure visibility and support long-term ROI on capex.

The April spike in ARR to $310 million is impressive but depends on the sustainability of a wider shift to enterprise-grade customers and country-level AI programs. The management pointed to the mid-term strategy of focusing on four pillars:

  1. AI-native startups (the largest segment today),
  2. frontier AI laboratories,
  3. multinational enterprises, and
  4. country-level AI factories.

Importantly, only the first segment currently contributes to ARR. This customer profile is accompanied by significant risk. AI-native companies face macro funding cycles, with the demand for GPUs potentially unraveling if venture capital collapses. Furthermore, their current revenue is predominantly U.S.-based, a risk in the face of continuing regulatory concern regarding chip exports, tariffs, and data sovereignty.

For its part, management has begun to seed strategically in other geographies. The Israel data center is a diversification wager in addition to being an early beachhead in sovereign AI deployments. Looking to exceed 1 gigawatt of capacity worldwide in its growth ambitions, Nebius is seeking to take advantage of demand from latency-sensitive or heavily regulated markets that hyperscalers have not yet fully penetrated.

EBIT Dreams, Cash Burn Reality: Nebius’s High-Stakes Path To Profitability

The firm's adjusted EBITDA is still in the red, but the management is guiding breakeven in H2 2025 on the strength of operating leverage in the infrastructure business as newer clusters ramp to full utilization. The more significant midterm goals are EBIT margins of 20–30% and revenue in the range of billions, presumably suggesting 5–7x revenue growth from today over the next three to four years.

However, this expansion will necessitate continued CapEx expenditures. Even as it drains its $1.44 billion cash reserves, the firm expects to tap the monetization of its minority holdings in ClickHouse (approximately ~$6 billion in value) and Toloka (supported by Jeff Bezos and Mikhail Parakhin). These deals will, in theory, pay for subsequent CapEx with little dilution, but the timing and availability of such exits are in question.

In contrast to other peers, Nebius maintains a conservative depreciation practice: full-year depreciation of assets in the field versus 5–6 years in the industry practice. This suppresses short-term GAAP profitability but arguably provides a more realistic portrayal of asset churn and requirements for recycling assets.

Importantly, the company currently carries no long-term debt and has not ruled out future public capital raises. The lack of leverage provides balance sheet flexibility, but leaves open the question of whether future growth will be funded internally, through asset sales, or via shareholder dilution.

Valuation Outlook: Pricing The Optionality, Discounting The Execution Risk

While Nebius has rebounded strongly, its valuation still looks attractive in the context of its growth and market position. Trading at a share price of $40, the company is valued at an enterprise value of ~$8 billion and a market value of $9.4 billion. Its April 2025 ARR of $310 million translates to an EV/ARR ratio of ~25.8x.

Notably, Nebius's ARR is expanding at almost 700% year-over-year, and management reaffirmed full-year ARR guidance of $750 million to $1 billion. If the company is able to make the midpoint of the range and is at $875 million of ARR in the tail-end of Q4, the forward EV/ARR multiple compresses to only ~9.1x, making the stock look substantially undervalued compared to the leading players with an average of 14.2x as at the end of 2024.

Achieving this will, however, require the continued ramping of infrastructure, deeper enterprise penetration, and more visibility on the durability of the revenue. The current multiple essentially prices skepticism over the durability of recurring revenue, customer concentration, and its ability to monetize its software stack, factors which can re-rate favorably in subsequent quarters if execution matches guidance.

Bottom Line

Despite a recent 80% rally, Nebius remains undervalued at ~9.1x forward ARR, well below AI cloud peers. With hypergrowth, global expansion, and untapped software monetization ahead, the stock offers asymmetric upside. Execution on ARR durability and capital efficiency could trigger a further re-rating over the next 2–4 quarters.

data.title: Kuaishou: Upgrade To Hold Amid Mixed Growth Outlook
data.description:
Kuaishou (KUASF) (OTCPK:KSHTY) reported 1Q25 results with revenue and non-GAAP earnings slightly ahead of expectations due to a beat on livestreaming, while e-commerce and online marketing both came largely in line with consensus. Operating metrics continue to hold up with DAUs growing at 3.6% y/y to 408mn.

Once again, management highlighted the progress Kuaishou made in AI and pointed out that Kling has generated over RMB150mn in revenue in the quarter. We believe this is largely in line with the sell-side consensus expectation of between RMB400mn and RMB600mn annual run-rate. The 2.0 version of Kling was launched in late April, supporting multi-modal editing features with several focused aspects, including motion quality, semantic responsiveness, and visual aesthetics, which can be applied to applications such as advertising, short videos, and smart devices.

Our thesis on Kuaishou is that its core business in live-streaming may face ongoing revenue challenges as viewers reduce discretionary spending, while online marketing and other services may experience revenue deceleration due to market maturity and competition within e-commerce. Given that much of Kuaishou’s business relies on e-commerce, China’s weak macro backdrop remains a key hurdle to revenue growth, and the ongoing investment in merchant retention could further exacerbate the margins outlook, given that the other e-commerce platforms, such as PDD and Alibaba, are also focused on merchant retention. (See: Kuaishou: E-Commerce Deceleration And Macro Headwinds Spell Growth Dilemma).

We recommend investors avoid Kuaishou and stick with higher-quality e-commerce plays. Within this sector, we like PDD given its global growth potential in Temu and JD given its exposure to electronics and home appliances segments, which, we believe, will continue to benefit from China’s ongoing subsidy campaign to drive consumption.

We upgrade Kuaishou to a HOLD from a SELL as we see limited downside risk from the current level (~5% downside) based on 1x forward revenue multiple.

We would be more bullish on the stock if there is a visible acceleration in GMV or improvement in execution in its overseas business. On the other hand, we would be more bearish on the stock should GMV decelerate further, coupled with weakening trends in online streaming. We are neutral on Kuaishou’s AI investment, given that China and the rest of the world have a plethora of AI models that could meet a general AI application demand, and the tech differentiation between Chinese AI models is limited, in our view.

Largely In-line Quarter Driven By Better-Than-Expected Online Streaming

Revenue of RMB 32.6bn was 1% ahead of consensus and 11% y/y growth. E-commerce GMV grew by 15.4%, largely in line with consensus to RMB 332.3bn, and once again paints a deceleration from the GMV growth in the prior year that delivered 20%-30% growth. Online marketing revenue growth of 8% was also in line with consensus at RMB 18bn, while live-streaming revenue grew 14.4% y/y, which came in well ahead of the 10% consensus estimate. At the group level, non-GAAP net income of RMB 4.58bn was in line with consensus.

Merchant support remains a key theme in e-commerce, which saw onboard merchants grow 30% y/y, which was largely driven by traffic support and a series of cost reduction policies for new merchants. Notably, Kuaishou offered traffic subsidies to new merchants to ensure their products and stores received proper exposure to viewers. The company’s “cold start” traffic coverage rate for new merchants now exceeds 95%, meaning almost all new merchants can receive guaranteed traffic support. On the cost side, new merchants benefit from zero-cost store opening, GMV incentives, and return shipping subsidies designed to lower the operational burden on the merchants. Finally, Kuaishou has been proactively promoting its AI tools to merchants to enhance operational efficiency. The pan-shelf-based GMV now accounts for 30% of the total GMV, with the number of daily active merchants increasing by 40% y/y while short video ecommerce GMV grew by 40% in the Q.

On the online marketing side, external marketing services continue to be the key driver for this segment compared with the previous years, in which internal marketing accounted for more than half of the ad revenue. To remind investors that Kuaishou's external marketing is the advertising service used by external brands that are looking to target a specific cohort on Kuaishou, while Kuaishou’s own merchants and KOLs use internal marketing to target users within Kuaishou. Ad revenue from short video was a key driver in the quarter, followed by local services that saw 50% y/y growth in the quarter. Total marketing spending through the Universal Auto X placement solution accounted for roughly 60% of total external marketing spending.

Finally, live-streaming was the surprise this quarter with better-than-expected revenue growth as Kuaishou strengthened several aspects of the business, including multi-host and group live-streaming with a number of partner talent agencies and streamers. We note that the number of talent agencies increased by over 25% while agency-based streamers increased by 40% y/y.

AI Is Playing A Key Role In The Investment Narrative

In late April, Kuaishou officially launched Kling AI 2.0, which introduces a multimodel visual language concept that enables users to integrate images, videos, voice, and motion paths to create and edit videos. This product is highly useful for KOLs and merchants who want to edit visual elements in generated videos with greater creative control and flexibility.

Kling AI generated RMB 150mn in revenue in the quarter, with 70% coming from paid prosumer subscriptions by video creators and marketing professionals. Kuaishou pointed out that this model has been adopted across various industries, including advertising, short films, and smart devices, with over 10,000 companies using the Kling API.

Little is known about the performance of Kling 2.0, but we do know that Kling 1.6 Pro was ranked first in image-to-video with a benchmark Arena ELO score over 1000. Kling 2.0 appears to excel at dynamism, aesthetics, and prompt fidelity, though not yet benchmarked publicly.

In short, we are upgrading Kuaishou to HOLD as our view has shifted to neutral, given the limited downside in share price. We would be more bullish on the stock if there is a clear acceleration in AI or its overseas growth, with rising overseas profitability.
